1: hamasa asher chazah Chavaquq hanavi: 'The oracle that Habakkuk the prophet saw.' The term massa (H4853, oracle/burden) is used for prophetic declarations of judgment — the word comes from the root nasa (to lift/carry), so the oracle is both 'lifted up' and a 'burden.' The verb chazah (saw/perceived, H2372) is the same root as chazon (vision) — prophetic reception is portrayed as a kind of seeing. Habakkuk is unique in that the oracle he 'saw' is largely composed of his own complaints to YHWH and YHWH's replies — a two-way dialogue rather than a unilateral proclamation. The name Habakkuk (Chavaquq, H2265) may derive from the verb chavaq (to embrace, H2263) — 'the one who embraces' or perhaps from an Akkadian plant name.
2: ad anatah YHWH shivati velo tishma: 'O LORD, how long shall I cry for help, and you will not hear?' The opening question establishes the book's theological problem: prophetic complaint (lament) addressed directly to YHWH. The verb za'aqti (I cry, H2199) is the standard distress-cry that YHWH is supposed to answer (Exod 3:7 — YHWH heard the cry of the Israelites in Egypt). Habakkuk's complaint is that this standard mechanism has broken down: he cries, but YHWH does not hear. Chamas (violence, H2555) — the keyword introduced here and repeated through the book — is the characteristic sin of unjust power (Gen 6:11; Ps 72:14). The prophet is overwhelmed by violence and YHWH seems inactive.
3: lama tasimeni raot veaval veshod veChamas lenegdi: 'Why do you make me see iniquity, and why do you idly look at wrong? Destruction and violence are before me; strife and contention arise.' The direct address 'why do you make me see' (lama tasimeni raot) — YHWH is not merely permitting evil but apparently compelling the prophet to witness it. Aven (iniquity/evil, H205) and amal (toil/trouble, H5999) are paired here and in several psalms of complaint. Riv (strife/lawsuit, H7379) and madon (contention, H4066) suggest the failure of the legal system — the dispute-resolution mechanisms of the covenant community have broken down.
4: al ken tapug Torah velo yetze lanetzach mishpat: 'So the law is paralyzed, and justice never goes forth.' The Torah (torah, H8451) is described as pugah (numb/benumbed/paralyzed, from pug = to grow numb/cold, H6313) — a vivid metaphor: the covenant instruction has lost its ability to function, like a limb that has gone numb. Mishpat (justice, H4941) 'never goes forth' (lo yetze... lanetzach) — the legal process produces no just outcome. Ki rasha maktir et hatzaddik — 'for the wicked surround the righteous, therefore justice goes forth perverted (meukkal, twisted/bent).' The root aqal (H5791, to twist/distort) describes justice deformed by power.
5: reu vagoyim vehabitu vehitamahu tamahu: 'Look among the nations, and see; wonder and be astounded. For I am doing a work in your days that you would not believe if told.' YHWH's first answer redirects the prophet's gaze to the nations (goyim). The fourfold imperative (look, see, wonder, be astounded) escalates the intensity of the divine announcement. Poal po'el biyemeichem (doing a work in your days) — YHWH is already at work in the geopolitical sphere. Paul quotes v5 in Acts 13:41 as a warning to those who reject the gospel: the 'incredible work' YHWH was doing through Babylon becomes a type of the 'incredible work' YHWH was doing in the resurrection — both defy expectation.
6: ki hineni meqim et haKasdim hagoy hamar vehanimer: 'For behold, I am raising up the Chaldeans, that bitter and hasty nation, who march through the breadth of the earth, to seize dwellings not their own.' Kasdim (Chaldeans/Babylonians, H3778) — the Neo-Babylonian Empire under Nabopolassar and Nebuchadnezzar II was the rising superpower of Habakkuk's day (late 7th century BCE). Marar (bitter, H4751) and maher (swift/hasty, H4116) — the two adjectives characterize Babylonian warfare as both aggressive and rapid. The verb meqim (I am raising up) — the same vocabulary YHWH uses for raising up judges (Judg 2:16) — is applied here to a pagan empire, demonstrating YHWH's sovereign use of all nations as instruments.
7: ayom veNora hu memenu mishpato uses'eto yetze: 'They are dreaded and fearsome; their justice and dignity go forth from themselves.' The Babylonians are not merely powerful but self-legislating — their law (mishpat) proceeds from themselves rather than from YHWH. This self-referential authority is the characteristic of imperial power that opposes YHWH's sovereignty: they define right and wrong, justice and honor, by their own standards. The verse sets up the theological problem: YHWH uses a nation whose very worldview is idolatrous self-sovereignty as the instrument of judgment on his own people.
8: veqalu mishenem usim mishoal zevavihem: 'Their horses are swifter than leopards, more fierce than the evening wolves.' The war-horse imagery (horses qalu = swift, H7043; chadu = fierce, H2300) builds a portrait of irresistible military force. Ze'evei erev (evening wolves, H2061) — wolves hunting at dusk when they are most active and dangerous. The Babylonian cavalry was renowned in the ancient Near East; Assyrian and Babylonian reliefs confirm the image of swift horse-mounted armies. Ufashu parashav — 'their horsemen spread out' — the flanking maneuver that characterized Babylonian military tactics.
9: kulo levias yavo megammat peneihem qadima: 'They all come for violence, all their faces forward.' The word megammat (H4041) is uncertain — possibly 'eagerness/striving' or related to the root for 'collective/mass.' The Babylonian advance is single-minded: kuloh levias (all of it for violence) — chamas appears again (cf. v2), now ascribed to the Babylonians as the instrument of judgment against Judah's own violence. Veyaspeh ka-chol shevi — 'he gathers captives like sand' — the simile of sand for incalculable numbers (cf. Gen 22:17) applied to the Babylonian mass deportations confirmed by archaeology.
10: vehu bamelakim yitkalal verozenim mishchaq lo: 'He scoffs at kings, and rulers are a laughingstock to him.' The Babylonian king's attitude to other monarchs: yitkalal (he scoffs/mocks, H7046) — the root of qalal (to treat lightly/despise). Rozenim mishchaq lo (rulers are a laughingstock to him) — the political humiliation of conquered kings was a standard feature of Babylonian imperial propaganda, documented in the Babylonian Chronicle. Vehu kol mivtzar yischaq vayisfor afar vayilkededah — 'he laughs at every fortress; he heaps up earth and takes it.' Siege-ramp construction (heaping up earth against walls) is confirmed archaeologically at multiple sites conquered by Nebuchadnezzar.
11: az chalaf ruach vayaavor veyashem zu kocho le'elohav: 'Then he sweeps by like the wind and goes on, guilty men, whose own might is their god.' The verse concludes the Babylonian portrait with a theological verdict: zu kocho le'elohav — 'this/he whose strength is his god.' The Babylonian deification of military power is the ultimate idolatry — making one's own capabilities the object of ultimate trust. This is the sin that will eventually destroy Babylon (2:4-5, 18-20): the proud man who makes himself his own god will fall by the very principle he lives by.
12: halo atta miqqedem YHWH elohai qedoshi lo namut: 'Are you not from everlasting, O LORD my God, my Holy One? We shall not die.' Habakkuk's second complaint begins with a confession: miqqedem (from everlasting, H6924) — YHWH's eternity as the ground of confidence. Qedoshi (my Holy One, H6918) — the title used extensively by Isaiah for YHWH; holiness (qedushah) means being set apart, transcendent, and morally pure. The confession 'we shall not die' (lo namut) is the foundation of Habakkuk's subsequent question: if YHWH is eternal and holy, how can he use a more wicked nation to punish a less wicked one? The word lemos'ath (for judgment/correction, H4941) — 'You have ordained them as a judgment'; this is Habakkuk's grudging acknowledgment of divine purpose.
13: tahorah einayim mire'ot ra vehabet el amal lo tuchal: 'You who are of purer eyes than to see evil and cannot look at wrong.' The theological core of Habakkuk's complaint: YHWH's holiness (tahorah einayim, pure eyes — the eyes of the Holy One cannot endure the sight of evil) creates a logical problem when YHWH uses evil Babylon as his instrument. Lama tabbit bogedim tacharish bivla rasha tzaddik mimenu — 'why do you look idly at traitors and remain silent when the wicked swallows up the man more righteous than he?' The comparative 'more righteous than he' (tzaddik mimenu) is the crux: Habakkuk does not claim Israel is innocent, only relatively more righteous than Babylon. YHWH's answer in ch2 addresses this directly.
14: vataaseh adam kidgei hayam kereems lo moshel bo: 'You make mankind like the fish of the sea, like crawling things that have no ruler.' The fish-and-crawling-things simile for humanity under Babylonian domination extends the prophetic critique: like fish with no shepherd, people are swept up in the nets of the powerful. Kidgei hayam (like fish of the sea) — the same image Ezekiel uses for Egypt's Pharaoh as a great dragon in the Nile (Ezek 29:3-5); Habakkuk applies it to all humanity under imperial domination. The absence of a ruler (lo moshel bo) is both a description of helplessness and an implicit complaint: where is the divine Ruler?
15: kulo bechangah he'ala: 'He brings all of them up with a hook.' The fishing imagery continues through vv15-17: hook (changah, H2443), net (cherem, H2764), and dragnet (mikhmoret, H4365) — three implements of mass capture. The Babylonian is the fisherman; nations are the catch. Yagel veyismach — 'he rejoices and is glad' — over the mass catch, the enemy celebrates his conquests with religious rites dedicated to his weapons (v16: he sacrifices to his net).
16: al ken yezabech le-cheremoh viqqater lemikhmartoh: 'Therefore he sacrifices to his net and makes offerings to his dragnet.' The Babylonian's idolatry is the worship of military technology: his net (= his military apparatus) becomes his god. Asher bashmen chelqo umaachalov bariah — 'for by them his portion is fat, and his food is rich.' The religious sanction of conquering power as divine favor is documented in Babylonian royal inscriptions: victories are thanksgivings to Marduk and the divine warrior gods. Habakkuk exposes this as the worship of tools — the net and dragnet given the honor that belongs to YHWH.
17: hal al ken yariq cheremoh vetamid laharog goyim lo yachmol: 'Is he then to keep on emptying his net and mercilessly killing nations forever?' The chapter ends with Habakkuk's second unanswered question: Will this go on indefinitely? The word tamid (continually/always, H8548) — the same word used for the daily (tamid) offering in the temple, now applied to the perpetual slaughter of nations. Lo yachmol (without mercy) — the absence of chemah (compassion/mercy). The question is not rhetorical despair but urgent prayer: this cannot be YHWH's permanent arrangement.
